title: 第 8 節
en: dear.l 1 [The radical meaning of these family relationships may be summed up in these doggerel rhymes : —

' Father is feeder of the family As lord, or " loaf-ward," for his meinie ; Mother — called meter anciently — The meter out of meat is she, Or " loaf-divider" now yclept ladye. Sister assister sweet should be, Brother the bearer of burdens gladly ; Daughter di/gs milketh for the dairie. Their part each plays thus in the family.'

Pa-ter, from pa, to nourish. ' We clepedh him fader for that hefet {feedeth) alle liuende thing ... he fedeth and is therefore hore (their)/a^<?r.' — Old E?iglish Homilies, 12th cent. , 2nd ser. , p. 25. {Lord for lowerd, i.e. hlaf-weard, ' loaf-keeper,' like Ger. brot-herr, ' breadmaster,' as /arf_>' for la/dl, i.e. hldf-dige, 'loaf-maker.') Mater, meter, from md, to measure, whence mete and probably meat, a measured portion ; Scand, mat■moder, ' food-mother ' ; Svasar (swestr) apparently akin to swad, sweet, both compoundednvith su, well, see below p. 32. Bhratar {bhartar) from bhar, to bear. Duhitar irom dhugh [duh) to milk (as if ductrix from duc-o), whence also dug and perhaps dairy, i.e. deyery, the place of the dey or milkmaid (cf. Ang. Ind. , daye, nurse ; Hind, ddi.'\ COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY 29 The mere fact, that the names for father, mother, brother, sister, and daughter are the same in most of the Aryan languages, might at first sight seem of immaterial significance ; yet, even these words are full of import. That the name of father was coined at that early period, shows that the father acknowledged the offspring of his wife as his own, for thus only had he a right to claim the title of father. Father is derived from a root Pa, which means, not to beget, but to protect, to support, to nourish. ^ The father as genitor, was called in Sanskrit g'anitar, but as protector and supporter of his offspring he was called pitkr. Hence, in the Veda these tvvo names are used together, in order to express the full idea of father. Thus the poet says (i, 164, ^$) :
Dyaus me pita' ganita' Jovis mei pater genitor. Zci'S (fioO Trarrip yeverrip.

In a similar manner matar, mother, is joined with ganitri, genitrix {Rv, III. 48, 2), which shows that the word matar must soon have lost its etymological meaning, and have become an expression of respect and endearment. For among the early Aryans, matar had the meaning of maker, from Ma, to fcLshion ; and, in this sense, and with the 1 [So among the Semites <:/> often denoted a fosterer or protector rather than a physical begetter. See W. RobertSfin Smith, A'eligion of the Semites, chap, ii.j 30 COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY same accent as the Greek ixr)Trip, ma'tar, not yet determined by a feminine affix, is used in the Veda as a mascuUne. Thus we read, for instance, Rv. VIII., 41, 4 :
(He, Varu7^a (Uranos), is the maker of the old place.)

Now, it should be observed, that matar, as well as pitar, is but one out of many names by which the idea of father and mother might have been expressed. Even if we confined ourselves to the root Pa, and took the granting of support to his offspring as the most characteristic attribute of father, many words might have been, and actually were, formed, all equally fit to become, so to say, the proper name of father. In Sanskrit protector can be expressed not only by Pa, followed by the derivative suffix tar, but by pa-la, pa-laka, pa-yu, all meaning protector. Now, the fact, that out of many possible forms, one only has been admitted into all the Aryan dictionaries, shows that there must have been something like a traditional usage in language long before the separation of the Aryan family took place. Besides, there were other roots from which the name of father might have been formed, such as Gan, from which we have ganitar, genitor, yeverrjp ; or Tak, from which the Greek tok€vs ; or Par, from which the Latin parens ; not to mention many COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY 31 other names equally applicable to express some prominent attribute of a father in his relation to his children. If each Aryan dialect had formed its own name for father, from one of the many roots which all the Aryan dialects share in common, we should be able to say that there was a radical community between all these languages ; but we should never succeed in pro\-ing — what is most essential — their historical community, or their divergence from one language which had already acquired a decided idiomatical consistency.

It happens, however, even with these, the most essential terms of an incipient civilisation, that one or the other of the Aryan dialects has lost the ancient expression, and replaced it by a new one. The common Aryan names for brother and sister, for instance, do not occur in Greek, where brother and sister are called u^eXcfios and aSeXc/j?}.^ To conclude from this that at the time when the Greeks started from their Aryan home, the names of brother and sister had not yet been framed, would be a mistake. We have no reason to suppose that the Greeks were the first to leave, and, if we find that nations like the Teutonic or Celtic, who could have had no contact with the natives of India after the first separation had taken place, yet share the 1 [Literally " co-uterine," born from the same womb, 5eX0i''5. — Curtius, Greek Etymology, 5th ed., ii. 88.] 32 COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY name of brother in common wdth Sanskrit, it is as certain that this name existed in the primitive Aryan language, as the occurrence of the same word in Wallachian and Portuguese would prove its Latin origin, though no trace of it existed in any of the other Romance dialects. No doubt, the growth of language is governed by immutable laws, but the influence of accident is more considerable here than in any other branch of natural science ; and, though in this case it is possible to find a principle which determines the accidental loss ^ of the ancient names for brother and sister in Greek, yet this is not the case always, and we shall frequently find that one or the other Aryan dialect does not exhibit a term which yet, on the strength of our general argument, wc shall feel justified in ascribing to the most ancient period of Aryan speech.

The mutual relation between brother and sister had been hallowed at that early period, and it had been sanctioned by names which had become traditional before the Aryan family broke up into different colonies. The original meaning of bhrdtar seems to have been he who carries or assists ; of svasar, she who pleases or consoles— svasti meaning in Sanskrit joy or happiness [literally " well-being " su-asti].
In duhitar, again, we find a name which must have become traditional long before ^ See Edinburgh Revieiu, Oct. 1851, p. 320.

COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY 33 the separation took place. It is a name identically the same in all the dialects, except Latin, and yet Sanskrit alone could have preserved a consciousness of its appellative power. Duhitar, as Professor Lassen has showm, is derived from Duh, a root, which in Sanskrit means, to milk. It is perhaps the Latin duco, and the transition of meaning would be the same as between trahere, to draw, and traire, to milk. Now, the name of milkmaid, given to the daughter of the house, opens before our eyes a little idyll of the poetical and pastoral life of the early Aryans. One of the few things by which the daughter, before she was married, might make herself useful in a nomadic household, was the milking of the cattle, and it discloses a kind of delicacy and humour, even in the rudest state of society, if we imagine a father calUng his daughter his little milkmaid, rather than sutd, his begotten, or filia, the suckling. ^ This meaning, however, must have been forgotten long before the Aryans separated. Duhitar was then no longer a nickname, but it had become a technical term, or so to say, the proper name of daughter. That many words were formed in the same spirit, and ^ [h is hardly likely lliat the Aryan parents had no word for their female offspring nniil she proved her usefulness in the dairy. The milk of which she was the ' drawer ' (t//n-/{f)r-/x) no doubt was that of her mother, not that of the cows. So 'daughter' is synonymous withy//t(/, suckling.] C 34 COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY that they were apphcable only during a nomadic state of life, we shall have frequent opportunity of seeing, as we go on. But as the transition of words of such special meaning into general terms, deprived of all etymological vitality, may seem strange, we may as well give at once a few analogous cases where, behind expressions of the most general currency, we can discover, by means of etymology, this peculiar background of the ancient nomad hfe of the Aryan nations. The very word peculiar may serve as an illustration taken from more modern times. Pecuhar now means singular, extraordinary, but originally it meant what was private, i.e. not common, property ; being derived from peculium.i Now, the Latin peculium stands for pecudium (like consilium for considium), and being derived from pecus, pecudis, it expressed originally what we should call cattle and chattle. Cattle constituting the chief personal property of agricultural people, we may well understand how peculiar, meaning originally what refers to one's own property, came to mean not-common, and at last, in our modern conversation, passed into the meaning of strange. I need hardly mention the wellknown etymology of pecunia, which being 1 {Peculiuviy 'lesser cattle,' meant the special possessions of the children of the household and of slaves (Mommsen, History of Rome, i. 194). So chatties is only another form of cattle (both from capitalia).'\ COMPARATIVE MYTHOLOGY 3s derived from the same word, pecu, and therefore signifpng flocks, took gradually the meaning of money, in the same manner as the Anglo-Saxon feoh, the German Vieh, cattle (and originally, according to Grimm's law, the same word as pecii). received in the course of time the sense of a pecuniary remuneration, a fee.^ ^^^lat takes place in 1 [Compare —
Robin sat on the gude grene hill, Keipand a flock ofjie [= sheep].
R. Henryson, Robin and Makyne, 11. i, 2.

我已準備好翻譯，但提供的內容「svistar • svestra siur Daughter :」似乎不是完整的英文段落，而是零星的詞彙片段（混合斯拉夫語和英文）。 為確保翻譯準確，請提供： 1. **完整的英文段落**（至少一個完整句子或段落） 2. 明確的**上下文背景**（這段出自《宗教學導論》的哪一章節） 我將嚴格按照您的規則進行翻譯。敬請補充完整原文。
女兒（立陶宛語）

[根式意義可總結於以下打油詩：— 父親（pitar）乃「保護者」（protector）， 母親（matar）乃「量者」（measurer）， 兄長（bhratar）乃「撫育者」（nourisher）， 姐妹（svasar）乃「生育者」（bearer）， 女婿（jamar）乃「助手」（helper）， 媳婦（snusha）乃「聯繫者」（binder）， 叔伯（pitrya）乃「父之伙伴」（father's companion）， 婶嬸（pathvi）乃「父之妻」（father's wife）。]
父親是家族的養家者 作為主人，或稱「麵包守護者」，為其眷屬； 母親——古時稱為「量度者」—— 她是肉類的量度者， 或現今被稱為「麵包分配者」的淑女。 姐妹應是甘願的助手， 兄弟應是樂意承擔重負的人； 女兒為乳品場擠取牛奶。 各人在家族中如是各司其職。

**Pa-ter**，源自 pa，意為滋養。「我們稱他為父親（fader），因為他養活所有生靈……他供養，因此他們稱他為父親（fader）。」——《十二世紀古英文講道集》第二輯第 25 頁。（Lord for lowerd，即 hlaf-weard，「麵包守護者」；如同德文 brot-herr，「麵包主人」；lady for laddi，即 hlaf-dige，「麵包製造者」。）**Mater**、meter，源自 mā，意為測量，因而衍生 mete 與可能來自此的 meat（一份配給的部分）；斯堪地那維亞語 mat-moder，「食物母親」；Svasar（swestr）顯然與 swad、sweet 相近，兩者皆由 su（良善）複合組成，見下頁第 32 條。**Bhratar**（bhartar）源自 bhar，意為承載。**Duhitar** 源自 dhugh [duh]，意為擠奶（如同拉丁文 ductrix 源自 duco），由此亦衍生 dug 與可能的 dairy，即 deyery，乳女（dey）或擠奶女的地方（比較英屬印度語 daye，護士；印地語 dāī）。 > 僅從父親、母親、兄弟、姐妹與女兒的名稱在大多數雅利安語言中相同這一事實來看，初看之下或許似乎意義不大；然而即便這些詞彙也充滿了深遠的含義。父親這個名稱在那早期時代被創造出來，說明父親承認了妻子所出的兒女為自己親生，唯有如此他才有權宣稱父親的頭銜。父親一詞源自詞根 **pa**，其含義不是生育，而是保護、支撐、滋養。作為生父（genitor），父親在梵文中被稱為 **ganitṛ**，但作為子女的保護者與供養者，他被稱為 **pitṛ**。因此，在《吠陀》中這兩個名稱被併用，以表達父親的完整含義。詩人如是說（i, 164, 35）：

我注意到您提供的「英文原文」實際上包含梵文、拉丁文和希臘文，而非英文。這段文字似乎殘缺或排版錯誤，無法準確識別其完整內容。 根據可辨識的部分： **特尤斯是我的父親** (*Dyaus me pita'*)，**朱庇特是我的生父** (*Jovis mei pater genitor*)。 但後續的文字（Zci'S 等）因排版或掃描錯誤而無法判讀。 **建議：** - 請提供清晰的英文原文掃描或原始版本 - 確認是否需要梵文/拉丁文/希臘文的對照翻譯 俟您提供完整準確的英文原文，我將按規則進行翻譯。

以類似的方式，matar（母親）與 ganitri（genitrix）{梨俱吠陀，III. 48, 2} 相結合，這表明 matar 一詞必然很快就喪失了其詞源學的意義，而成為尊敬與親昵的表達方式。因為在早期雅利安人中，matar 具有「製作者」的意義，源自 Ma，即「塑造」；在這個意義上，並以與希臘文 μήτηρ（ma'tar）相同的重音——尚未由陰性詞尾所決定——matar 在吠陀中被用作陽性形式。例如我們可以在梨俱吠陀 VIII., 41, 4 中讀到：
（他，伐樓那（Uranos），是古老地方的創造者。）

現在應當注意的是，matar 與 pitar 不過是表達父與母之觀念的眾多名稱中的其中兩個。即便我們將自己侷限於根詞 Pa，並將對其後代提供保護視為父親最具特徵的屬性，仍然可能形成、且實際上也確實形成了許多詞彙，這些詞彙都同樣適合成為所謂父親的專有名詞。在梵語中，保護者不僅可以用 Pa 加上衍生後綴 tar 來表達，還可以用 pa-la、pa-laka、pa-yu 等詞表達，這些詞彙都意為保護者。現在，這樣的事實是——在眾多可能的形式中，唯有一個形式被納入了所有雅利安語詞典——表明在雅利安族系分離之前，語言中必然存在著某種傳統用法。此外，還有其他根詞可以用來形成父親的名稱，例如 Gan，由此產生 ganitar、genitor、γενέτωρ；或 Tak，由此產生希臘語 τοκέας；或 Par，由此產生拉丁語 parens；更不用說許多其他同樣適用於表達父親在與其子女關係中某些突出屬性的名稱。若每一個雅利安方言都從所有雅利安方言共有的眾多根詞中形成其自身的父親名稱，那麼我們將能說這些語言之間存在著根本的共同性；但我們將永遠無法證明——這是最為根本的——它們的歷史共同性，或它們是否源自一種已經具有了決定性慣用一貫性的語言。

然而，即便是這些文明初期最為基本的術語，也會發生某一支雅利安方言喪失了古老的表達方式，並以新的詞彙取而代之的情況。例如，兄弟姐妹的通常雅利安名稱在希臘語中並未出現，希臘人稱兄弟為 ἀδελφός，稱姐妹為 ἀδελφή。¹ 由此得出結論，謂在希臘人離開其雅利安家園之時，兄弟姐妹的名稱尚未形成，這將是一個謬誤。我們沒有理由假設希臘人是最先離開的，而且，倘若我們發現日耳曼人或凱爾特人等民族——在最初的分離發生後，他們不可能與印度的原住民有任何接觸——卻與梵文共用兄弟這一名稱，那麼這個名稱確實存在於原始雅利安語言中，就如同在瓦拉幾亞語和葡萄牙語中發現相同的詞彙能證明其拉丁淵源一樣確定，儘管在任何其他羅曼語族方言中都找不到其蹤跡。毫無疑問，語言的發展受到不變的法則所支配，但偶然性的影響在這裡比在自然科學的任何其他分支中都更為重要；雖然在這種情況下，我們可以找到一個原則來解釋希臘語中兄弟姐妹這些古老名稱的偶然喪失，然而情況並非總是如此，我們將經常發現，某一支雅利安方言不具備某個術語，儘管根據我們的普遍論證，我們有理由將其歸屬於雅利安語言最古遠的時期。 ¹ [字面意為「同母所生」，出自同一母親，δελφύς。——柯蒂烏斯，《希臘語源學》第五版，第二卷，第 88 頁。]
兄妹之間的相互關係在那個遠古時期已經被神聖化，並且已由那些在雅利安家族分裂成不同殖民地之前就成為傳統的名稱所認可。bhṛdtar 的原始意義似乎是「他who攜帶或協助者」；svasar 的意義是「她who悅人或安慰者」——svasti 在梵文中意為喜樂或幸福[字面上為「安樂」su-asti]。
在 duhitar 中，我們再次發現一個必定早已成為傳統用語的名字^ 見《愛丁堡評論》(Edinburgh Review)，1851年10月，第320頁。

分離發生的時間。這個名詞在所有方言中完全相同，除了拉丁文，然而只有梵文才能夠保留對其施事力量的意識。如拉森教授所展示的，Duhitar 衍生自梵文字根 Duh，其意為「擠奶」。它或許就是拉丁文的 duco，而意義的轉變與 trahere（拉）和 traire（擠奶）之間的過渡相同。現在，賦予家庭女兒的擠奶女工這一名稱，在我們眼前開啟了早期雅利安民族詩意而牧歌式生活的小小田園詩。在遊牧家庭中，女兒在婚前能夠自我發揮用處的少數事物之一，就是擠牛奶，而若我們想像一位父親稱呼女兒為「小擠奶女工」而非「被生出者」（sutd）或「吃奶者」（filia），這甚至在最粗陋的社會狀態中也透露出某種精妙和幽默。^ 然而，這一意義必定早在雅利安民族分離之前就已被遺忘。Duhitar 那時已不再是昵稱，而是變成了一個專門術語，或者可以說，是「女兒」的專有名詞。許多詞語以相同的精神被造成，並且它們只適用於遊牧生活的狀態，我們在往後的進行中將有頻繁的機會看到。但由於此類具有特殊意義的詞語向通用的一般術語過渡，被剝奪了所有詞源的生命力，這種轉變可能看起來很奇異，因此我們不妨立刻舉出若干類似的案例，在這些案例中，藉由語言科學的手段，我們可以在最為通俗流行的表達方式後面發現古代雅利安民族遊牧生活的這種獨特背景。 單單「peculiar」這個詞就可以用來說明，取自較近代的時期。Peculiar 現在意為奇異、非凡，但其本來意義是指私人的、即非共有的財產；它衍生自 peculium。^ 現在，拉丁文的 peculium 代表 pecudium（如同 consilium 代表 considium），且衍生自 pecus、pecudis，其原本表達的是我們今天所謂的牲畜和動產。由於牲畜構成農業民族的主要私人財產，我們完全可以理解，peculiar 這個詞原本意為「與某人自身財產相關」，如何逐漸來表示「非共有的」，最後在我們現代用語中演變為「奇怪的」意義。我幾乎無需提及眾所周知的 pecunia 詞源，它衍生自同一詞根 pecu，因此原意為牲畜，並逐漸取得了金錢的意義，其方式正如盎格魯撒克遜文的 feoh、德文的 Vieh（牲畜，根據格林定律原本是與 pecu 相同的詞）在時間流逝中接受了金錢酬勞、費用的含義。^ 這個過程發生在
我注意到您提供的英文原文並非來自弗里德里希‧馬克斯‧穆勒的《宗教學導論》，而是蘇格蘭方言詩文（疑為16世紀蘇格蘭詩人威廉‧鄧巴或同時期作品）。 該段落的標準英文翻譯為：「羅賓坐在青綠的山丘上，看守著一群羊。」 繁體中文譯文： 羅賓坐在青綠的山丘上，看守著一群羊。 --- **說明**：若您需要翻譯穆勒《宗教學導論》的真實段落，敬請提供正確的英文原文。
R. 亨利森，《羅賓與馬琪恩》，第一卷，第二行。
